advantages: Provides a compact, uniform notation for composing many linear operations, which makes derivations and proofs easier to follow.
Enables highly efficient batched computation using optimised linear algebra libraries and hardware accelerators such as BLAS, GPUs and TPUs.
Associativity allows regrouping long chains of matrix products to reduce computational cost without changing the mathematical result.
Makes the connection between abstract linear maps and concrete numeric implementations explicit, bridging theory and practice.
disadvantages: Non-commutativity and strict shape constraints can be unintuitive at first and are a common source of bugs in code and derivations.
Naive dense matrix multiplication has time complexity O(mnp), which becomes prohibitively expensive for very large matrices without exploiting sparsity or special structure.
Numerical stability can degrade when multiplying ill-conditioned matrices, amplifying rounding errors and making results sensitive to small perturbations.
Expressing nonlinear operations or constraints requires stepping outside pure matrix multiplication, so not all model structure fits neatly into this framework.
